Scientists at the University of Cambridge have developed a new approach to storing solar energy that could revolutionise how we power our homes and cities.

The breakthrough involves a liquid organic molecule that can absorb sunlight and store it as chemical energy for months at a time, releasing it as heat when needed.

Unlike conventional batteries, the system degrades very little over time and requires no rare earth minerals, making it potentially far cheaper to produce at scale.

The lead researcher described it as a kind of rechargeable fuel, one that could be pumped through pipes and stored in tanks like conventional heating oil but generated entirely from sunlight.

The team is now working with industrial partners to develop a prototype system suitable for domestic installation, with trials expected to begin within two years.

If successful, the technology could complement existing solar panel installations by solving the fundamental problem of intermittency, storing summer sunshine for use in winter months when demand for heating is highest.

The research was funded by the European Research Council and published this week in the journal Nature Energy.
